original_abstract: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are frontrunners for the electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR), offering a viable pathway toward achieving carbon neutrality. Despite the proliferation of AgCu catalyst designs, the fundamental question of how these materials evolve under operating conditions remains largely unanswered. This lack of insight into their structural stability masks the nature of the true active sites and hinders rational catalyst development. In this work, we synthesized both intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on carbon paper electrodes to systematically track their morphological and chemical transformation during CO2RR. Using time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping, we demonstrate that Cu atoms exhibit significant mobility under reaction conditions; they migrate to the catalyst surface, leach into the environment, and eventually re-agglomerate as distinct particles. Furthermore, we found that both intermixed and phase-separated precursors gravitate toward a common thermodynamic state characterized by distinct Ag-rich (Ag0.88Cu0.12) and Cu-rich (Ag0.05Cu0.95) grains. This persistent phase separation occurs across both the bulk and surface of the catalyst, emphasizing the critical role of Ag–Cu phase boundaries in governing CO2RR activity. Complementary operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy further identifies metallic Cu as the primary catalytically active species. By mapping these dynamic changes, this study provides a comprehensive framework for understanding the chemical and structural life cycle of bimetallic catalysts during electrolysis.
humanized_abstract: Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are one of the most commonly used catalysts in the electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR) toward realization of a carbon-neutral society. Despite a large number of studies on the design of AgCu catalysts, very little is known on the evolution behavior of these catalysts, which leads to uncertainty on the active sites of the catalyst. Here, we prepared intermixed and phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles on carbon paper and investigated their structural evolutions in CO2RR process using time-sequential electron microscopy and elemental mapping techniques. Our results demonstrate that the Cu atoms exhibited a large diffusion behavior during the electrochemical process and dissolution of Cu into electrolyte and re-precipitation of Cu particles. The bimposed on the as-prepared intermixed and phase-separated AgCu precursors always tend toward a stable state with the formation of the Ag0.88Cu0.12 and Ag0.05Cu0.95 phases and remain stable with the coexistence of Ag-rich and Cu-rich grains in both the bulk and surface of catalyst. These results confirm that the Ag–Cu phase boundaries play a crucial role in the CO2RR activity. Operando high-energy-resolution X-ray absorption spectroscopy also confirmed that the metallic Cu is active sites in the CO2RR process.
